text: Five trocars are placed for the robotic Whipple procedure, including a 12mm trocar at the umbilicus and another at the right midclavicular line. Three additional ports are 8mm robotic cannulas. The abdomen is inspected for metastatic disease. The round ligament is taken down and used as a vascularized pedicle. The gallbladder is sutured to the anterior abdominal wall with a V-lock suture in a running fashion, providing exposure of the portohepatous for dissection without a Nathanson retractor.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament using a vessel sealant device, mobilizing the stomach along its greater curvature both proximally and distally. The mobilization continues posteriorly along the distal antrum and pylorus, and the lesser curvature is also mobilized, extending just distal to the pylorus.

The surgeon transects the duodenum using a robotic stapler just distal to the pylorus. An intraoperative ultrasound is utilized to identify key vascular structures and assess the proximity of the tumor to these structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects and suture ligates the right gastric artery. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected free, controlled with hemlock clips, and divided. The inferior border and neck of the pancreas are meticulously dissected and mobilized, with umbilical tape placed posteriorly for traction.

The surgeon transects the neck of the pancreas using monopolar scissors, with saline irrigation to reduce tissue charring. A window is created in the transverse mesocolon to the left of the middle colic vessels, revealing the jejunum. The jejunum is then transected 15 centimeters distal from the ligament of Treitz using the robotic stapler.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uncinate process away from the superior mesenteric vessels, suture ligating branches from the superior mesenteric vein with six sutures. The cystic duct and cystic artery are clipped and divided, followed by transection of the common hepatic duct just above the cystic duct takeoff.
The surgical specimen is placed into a specimen retrieval bag and extracted through the umbilical trocar site, then sent to pathology for frozen section analysis.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase with a two-layer end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy. The posterior layer is sutured with a 5-0 monofilament in a running fashion, while a small enterotomy is made in the jejunum. The anastomosis is created using interrupted 6-0 monofilament sutures, typically over a small 7 or 5 French pancreatic duct stent. The anterior layer is completed with an additional 5-0 running monofilament suture, and the entire anastomosis is wrapped using the round ligament pedicle flap.

The surgeon creates a hepatico-jejunostomy approximately ten to fifteen centimeters downstream from the pancreatic jejunostomy using five-oh monofilament sutures in a running or interrupted fashion. The gallbladder is dissected free from the gallbladder fossa and detached from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs an anticholic duodenal jejunostomy approximately fifty centimeters from the biliary anastomosis, using absorbable monofilament sutures in a running fashion.
Following the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon removes the remaining specimens and places a suction drain in the right upper quadrant, adjacent to the bile duct and pancreatic anastomosis.
